pushing_hardest_on: The AI feature ships with no Behavior Contract, kill-switch, or eval bar
The core mechanism — that typing is the bottleneck — is assumed, not evidenced
Metrics have a real outcome but no quality counter-metric

axes.reason: Quantified pain from two independent sources — a measured reply time and an exit-survey ranking — not an asserted category description.
axes.quote: support measured an average first-reply time of six hours, and exit surveys named slow replies as the top complaint
axes.what_id_want_to_see: Already strong — a breakdown of where the six hours go would also de-risk the Solution mechanism.

axes.reason: A real if/then/because hypothesis, but the load-bearing mechanism — that typing is the slowest step — is assumed rather than shown.
axes.quote: Drafting the reply will cut reply time because typing is the slowest step in handling a ticket.
axes.what_id_want_to_see: Evidence that composition, not triage or lookup, is the bottleneck — e.g. a time-and-motion breakdown of the six-hour reply time.

axes.reason: Clean IN / OUT boundary, but no CUT history or v1 trade-off rationale.
axes.quote: In scope: English-language email tickets. Out of scope: chat, phone, and non-English tickets in v1.
axes.what_id_want_to_see: A CUT list — anything considered for v1 and dropped, and why.

axes.reason: Median reply time is a genuine outcome, but draft-acceptance is an output, and there is no quality counter-metric, baseline, or threshold.
axes.quote: We will track median first-reply time and the percentage of drafts sent without edits.
axes.what_id_want_to_see: A baseline (six hours today), a target and timeframe, and a counter-metric — re-open or CSAT — so a faster wrong reply can't read as success.

axes.reason: A feature within an existing product; no new pricing decision is at stake.
A feature PRD within an established product; strategic positioning is out of scope for this doc.
axes.what_id_want_to_see: n/a — strategy is not re-decided here.

axes.reason: A staged internal rollout gated on a metric, but the expansion trigger is vague ('once reply time improves') with no number.
axes.quote: We will enable Copilot for ten percent of agents first, then expand once reply time improves.
axes.what_id_want_to_see: A concrete expansion gate — the reply-time delta and the draft-quality bar that must be met before widening rollout.

axes.reason: Stage is clear — an addition to a mature, daily-used console — and the reliability-first framing fits it.
axes.quote: This adds to our existing support console, which hundreds of agents already use every day.
axes.what_id_want_to_see: Already strong — an SLO for draft latency would round out the at-scale framing.

axes.reason: An LLM-backed feature with no Behavior Contract (GOOD/BAD/REJECT), no fallback or kill-switch for low-confidence drafts, no eval metric or ship bar, and no red-team list — 'monitor and improve over time' is not a launch control. A wrong drafted reply to a customer is a trust harm, not a UX nit.
axes.quote: We will monitor draft quality and improve the model over time as we gather agent feedback.
axes.what_id_want_to_see: A Behavior Contract with GOOD/BAD/REJECT exemplars; a low-confidence fallback (suppress the draft); offline + online eval metrics with a ship bar; and a red-team list (PII leakage, confidently-wrong policy answers, prompt injection from ticket text).

axes.reason: Names the single most important risk (over-trust of wrong drafts) but offers no mitigation and no broader pre-mortem.
axes.quote: The main risk is that agents over-trust a draft and send a customer the wrong information.
axes.what_id_want_to_see: A mitigation for over-trust (confidence signals, mandatory-edit for policy answers) and a pre-mortem covering model outage, PII leakage, and injection.

weakest_claims.claim: Model quality can be handled by monitoring and improving over time.
weakest_claims.quote: We will monitor draft quality and improve the model over time as we gather agent feedback.
followups: What is the GOOD/BAD/REJECT contract and the eval bar the model must clear before it reaches a single customer?
On a low-confidence draft, what happens — is there a kill-switch, or does the agent still see it?

weakest_claims.claim: Typing is the slowest step, so drafting cuts reply time.
weakest_claims.quote: Drafting the reply will cut reply time because typing is the slowest step in handling a ticket.
followups: Is composition actually the bottleneck, versus triage, lookup, or approvals?
If agents must read and verify every draft, does net handle-time fall or just shift?

strengths: A real falsifiable hypothesis with a stated mechanism
Honest stage framing on a mature, high-volume surface
must_dos: Add a Behavior Contract, low-confidence fallback, eval bar, and red-team list before any customer sees a draft
Add a quality counter-metric so a faster wrong reply can't read as success
Evidence that typing — not triage or lookup — is the reply-time bottleneck

limits: The model, prompt, and data-handling details are summarized in one section — a linked technical design may carry the eval and safety specifics not visible here
